summary:
Brittle bone disease primarily results from defects in type I collagen production; these include direct mutations affecting protein structure and indirect effects through broader metabolic disorders impacting collagen processing. Key aspects involve abnormalities at various stages leading to compromised structural integrity of bones. To summarize:

- Defects mainly stem from problems synthesizing type I collagen.
- Both gene-level changes directly altering collagens and wider systemic influences reducing their effectiveness contribute significantly. Sentence count matches requirement exactly while focusing solely on clinically pertinent information regarding causative factors behind brittleness observed across different forms of skeletal fragility disorder. Agent2 concludes based largely upon core biological mechanisms underpinning defective collagen creation which are central determinants influencing overall susceptibility towards developing symptoms characteristic of such syndromes including frequent fractures among others associated signs pointing toward underlying molecular pathology rooted deeply within connective tissue architecture itself
